items.title: Bomb blast at Moscow apartment building kills at least one person | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Some breaking news out of Moscow: a blast in the lobby of a luxury apartment block has killed at least one person and wounded four others. 
The bomb detonated just as a man with bodyguards entered the lobby of the complex. Russian media called the blast an "assassination attempt" and identified the suspected target as Armen Sarkisyan, who is reportedly in a critical condition. Ukraine has accused him of aiding Russia's war effort in it's eastern region of Donetsk.

items.title: Trump, Netanyahu to meet in Washington: What to expect | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu is in Washington to meet with US President Donald Trump. The two are set to discuss a wide range of topics including the ceasefire with Hamas and Israeli concerns over Iran's regional influence.
Netanyahu is the first foreign leader to visit Trump in his second term. He said he hopes to secure the White House's backing to 'redraw the map of the Middle East' in Israel's favor. The two leaders have enjoyed good relations in the past, but Trump has said he wants to end wars in the Middle East, not encourage more fighting. 
This meeting could be a test of the ties between the two leaders, and an early indicator of Trump's approach to the Middle East.

00:00 Netanyahu, Trump to meet in Washington, DC
03:52 DW's Emily Gordine reports from Jerusalem

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Huge demonstration in Berlin after conservative CDU votes with AfD to pass motion in parliament
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Over 150,000 people have gathered in Berlin to call on Germany's other political parties not to cooperate with the far right AfD. It comes after the conservative CDU voted with the AfD to pass a motion in parliament. Critics say it broke a longstanding convention in German politics against working with the far right. On Saturday, a similar march in Hamburg drew some 220-thousand people.  The AfD is projected to poll strongly in German federal elections later this month.

items.title: Trump's aid freeze sends shock waves across Africa | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Many African governments and aid organizations have reacted with shock and confusion after US President Donald Trump ordered a freeze and review of foreign aid.
Despite a reversal on life-saving aid, particularly for HIV treatments, it remains unclear what will happen after the review. Questions remain about what else will be funded and who exactly will receive it. 
The US is the world's largest single aid donor and a key provider of humanitarian assistance to Africa. So a freeze or reduction in aid could have devastating consequences for millions, and put lives at risk.
Last year, the United States provided nearly $7 billion in humanitarian aid to sub-Saharan Africa, according to USAID. This funding covered a wide range of support, including HIV prevention, malaria treatment, agricultural assistance, and initiatives to combat the effects of climate change.
In 2024, UN agencies received the largest share of funding, totaling over $3 billion, followed closely by US government bodies and US-affiliated NGOs.

00:00 Trump orders freeze on foreign aid
03:24 DW speaks with Dr. Catherine Kyobutungi, Executive Director of the African Population and Health Research Center in Uganda

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Fears grow that fighting in DR Congo could lead to regional war | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Foreign ministers of the G7 Group have strongly condemned the rebel offensive in the Democratic Republic of Congo. The president of Burundi has also warned that the escalating conflict could lead to a regional war. M23 rebels are trying to advance southwards, days after capturing the key eastern city of Goma. 
Life in Goma is gradually returning to normal despite the rebel takeover. Some markets are reopening and traders are entering the city on roads that were blocked by fighting. But cash and fuel are scarce, and many people are still cut off from food and water. And thousands of displaced people have fled camps on the outskirts of the city, citing a lack of security.

00:00 Life returns to normal in Goma, with M23 in charge
00:45 Journalist Al Katanty Djaffar reports from Goma

items.title: Canada imposes 25% tariffs on US imports in retaliation to Trump's tariff plan | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Canadian Prime Minister Justin Trudeau says Canada will impose counter tariffs of 25% on billions of dollars worth of US goods. The move is in retaliation to US tariffs on Candadian goods unvieled by US President Donald Trump. He signed an order to levy 25 percent tariffs on goods from both Canada and Mexico.  Trump will also slap 10% levies on imports from China. These are due to take effect on Tuesday.  DW talks to Toronto-based political commentator, Sean Mallen. 

00:00 The US puts tariffs on imports from its biggest trading partners, Canada, Mexico and China. Canada announces counter tariffs.

items.title: Gazans seeking treatment in Egypt worry they won't be allowed to return | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>The Rafah border crossing between Gaza and Egypt has opened for the first time in nearly nine months. Gaza's Hamas-run health ministry said 50 sick and wounded children were among the first to head to Egypt to receive medical treatment. Israel's retaliation for the October 7th terror attacks has put most of Gaza's hospitals out of operation, creating a dire situation for those in need of medical care. 
DW talks to Laura Blumenfeld, a Middle East Analyst at Johns Hopkins University.

00:00 Sick and wounded children among the first Gazans to go to Egypt for treatment as border reopens. 
02:24 Middle East Analyst Laura Blumenfeld tells DW where she thinks phase 2 of ceasefire negotiations might go

items.author: DW News
items.content:

The Israeli military has confirmed that all three men are back in Israel. Israel is freeing dozens of Palestinian prisoners in exchange.

items.title: How Trump's tariffs will impact the global economy | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>US President Donald Trump has announced he is imposing tariffs on the US' three largest trading partners - Canada, Mexico and China - starting on Saturday. 
The White House says it will implement a 25 percent tax on imports from Canada and Mexico, and a 10 percent tax on goods from China. Trump has also threatened the EU with similar measures, but has offered no clear timeline. Trump had pledged the measure to boost local manufacturing in the US, and to pressure its neighbors to do more to stop the flow of migrants and illicit drugs into the country.
Both Canada and Mexico have said they're ready to retaliate. But analysts say the US tax will likely deal a heavy blow to Mexico - Latin America's second-largest economy.

00:00 US imposes tariffs on its bigest trading partners
03:53 DW speaks with Kimberly Clausing at the UCLA school of law
08:31 DW speaks with John Kirton, professor of political science at the University of Toronto

items.title: Germany: Lawmakers reject controversial bill intended to reform immigration | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Lawmakers in Germany have rejected a controversial bill on immigration reform. The draft legislation was proposed by the conservative opposition CDU party, and sparked outrage not necessarily because of its content, but because the only chance for it to pass would have been with backing from the far-right Alternative for Germany party. A successful vote would have marked the first time in Germany's post-war history that federal legislation is pushed through with the support of the far right. It did not pass. And still many ask: is the genie now out of the bottle?

Chapters:
0:00 The vote, the consequences, and the other vote
3:34 Matthew Karnitschnig, Euroactiv Editor-in-Chief

items.title: Colombia's anti-drug operations thrown into jeopardy following US deportations | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Donald Trump has shaken up US policy since taking office at the start of last week. Mass deportations of irregular migrants and the freezing of foreign aid budgets are affecting countries well beyond the US borders. South-American Colombia especially has been at the sharp end of those policies. After the Trump presidency forced the government in Bogot√° to accept deported migrants the country's anti-drug operations are now in jeopardy. 

A large part of Colombia's fleet of anti-narcotics helicopters has been grounded after Washington ended aid payments. The aircraft belong to the United States and the US also pays for their maintenance. Colombia is the biggest cocaine producer in the world with most of the drug smuggled to the United States.

items.title: Brexit five years on: The reason few are celebrating | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>It's five years to the day that the United Kingdom left the European Union. That happened after a divisive vote that split the electorate down the middle and left the nation divided. The social bruises from that battle may have faded. But it's fair to say this fifth anniversary isn't been marked by huge street parties either. 

Polls say a majority of British people now think Brexit was a mistake. But there's also very little support for rejoining the European Union. Too great was the trauma of the polarization and arguments surrounding the Brexit debate.

Five years on, people and businesses are still wrestling with the economic, social and cultural aftershocks. Here's the CEO of one small business with customers in the EU.

Chapters:
0:00 Brexit, five years on and mostly forgotten?
1:28 Catherine Barnard, Law Professor, Trinity College

items.title: *Listen* to air traffic control right before the Washington DC plane crash | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>This is the moment air traffic controllers appeared to warn a military helicopter (PAT25) before it crashed into a plane (CRJ) in Washington DC, causing an explosion. Passengers and crew on both aircraft are presumed to be dead.

items.title: Germany‚Äôs unpopular chancellor: Can Olaf Scholz turn things around? | Berlin Briefing Podcast
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Olaf Scholz‚Äôs government collapsed after months of infighting and deadlock that left him the most unpopular chancellor since German reunification. But now he‚Äôs attempting what would be a historic comeback in the upcoming elections. Why did Scholz become so unpopular? What was the interplay between the Russia‚Äôs war in Ukraine, Germany‚Äôs unfolding economic malaise, and political dysfunction? And do the emerging battles over migration and the role of the AfD in German politics provide Scholz with an opportunity to make that comeback? DW‚Äôs Richard Walker, Nina Haase and Michaela K√ľfner break down the Scholz years in this edition of Berlin Briefing.

CHAPTERS
00:00 Introduction
03:26 Merkel 2.0?
08:15 Anti-capitalist?
12:28 The 'Zeitenwende'
24:48 Economic crisis
31:45 Coalition explodes
41:45 Comeback?
47:10 Who to watch

items.title: The M23 rebels have declared that they will march to Kinsasha | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>M23 rebels in the Democratic Republic of Congo are vowing to march on the capital, Kinshasa, after making further advances in the east of the country. The armed group M23 is backed by Rwanda and recently captured most of the eastern provincial capital of Goma. The UN says it is 'deeply concerned' over the group's advance to the south.

0:00 Many cilvilians in DRC injured and displaced by ongoing fights
2:16 Jonas Gerding, DW correspondent in Kinshasa 
6:15 Shelley Thakral, World Food Program in Kinshasa

items.title: Investigations into how the mid-air collision happened are underway | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>There are no survivors from a mid-air collision between a passenger jet and a military helicopter near Washington DC The two aircraft with a total of 67 passengers and crew on board crashed into the freezing Potomac River. Emergency crews have recovered dozens of bodies, and investigators have found the black box recorders from the crashed airliner.
At a press briefing President Donald Trump made some early-stage assumptions as to what may have caused the aviation crash.

0:00 investigations underway into cause of mid-air aviation crash in Washington DC
2:45 Matthew Cappucci, Journalist and eye-witness, Washington DC

items.title: Kallas: EU has opportunity to be "big geopolitical player" | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>The EU's foreign policy chief has told DW she expects the US will respect the sovereignty of Greenland, after Donald Trump's threats to take the Danish-controlled Territory. Kaja Kallas also said there haven't been any discussions about stationing EU troops there.
